title: PriorityQueue (Java's Built-In Heap)
description: Going deeper than the Queue/Deque preview -- PriorityQueue's min-heap default, custom ordering with Comparator, and why peek/poll are O(1)/O(log n).
objectives: Understand PriorityQueue as Java's built-in heap, defaulting to a min-heap
Use a custom Comparator to build a max-heap
Know peek/poll/offer's time complexities and why they differ

sections.text: In Queue and Deque, we saw PriorityQueue always returns the smallest element first, regardless of insertion order, and promised a full lesson once Heap arrived. Today we go deeper into HOW and WHY.

A tree-based data structure where each parent is ordered relative to its children (smaller for a min-heap, larger for a max-heap), keeping the smallest (or largest) element always accessible at the root.

sections.title: PriorityQueue's core operations
rows: Returns the smallest element WITHOUT removing it
Removes AND returns the smallest element

sections.text: peek() is O(1) because the smallest element is ALWAYS at the root, instantly accessible. poll() is O(log n) because after removing the root, the heap must reorganize itself to restore its ordering property -- this reorganizing is exactly what Manual Heap Implementation (next lesson) shows in full detail.
3. Building a Max-Heap with a Comparator
PriorityQueue only gives a min-heap by default. For a max-heap (largest first), supply a Comparator -- recall Comparable vs Comparator -- that reverses the natural order.

Collections.reverseOrder() (from the Collections Utility Class lesson) provides a Comparator that reverses natural ordering -- passed into the PriorityQueue constructor, it flips the heap's behavior from smallest-first to largest-first.
4. A Real Use Case: Kth Largest Element

sections.title: Why this finds the 2nd largest
points: The heap is only ever allowed to hold k=2 elements -- whenever it grows past that, the SMALLEST is discarded
After processing all of {3,2,1,5,6,4}, only the 2 LARGEST values survive in the heap: {5,6}
The min-heap's root (peek()) is the smaller of those two survivors -- exactly the 2nd largest overall: 5

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Assuming PriorityQueue's toString() or iteration order reflects sorted order -- only poll() repeatedly gives elements in true heap order; the internal array is NOT fully sorted.
Forgetting PriorityQueue defaults to a min-heap -- needing a max-heap requires an explicit Comparator like Collections.reverseOrder().
Confusing peek()'s O(1) with poll()'s O(log n) -- removing requires reorganizing the heap, looking does not.
Using PriorityQueue when you actually need strict FIFO order -- it always prioritizes by value, ignoring insertion order entirely, unlike a plain Queue.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: Does PriorityQueue give elements in sorted order if you iterate over it directly (not via poll())?
questions.answer: No. Only repeatedly calling poll() guarantees elements come out in heap order (smallest/largest first). The internal array backing the heap is not fully sorted, so direct iteration order is unspecified.

questions.question: How do you make a PriorityQueue behave as a max-heap instead of the default min-heap?
questions.answer: Supply a Comparator that reverses the natural ordering, such as new PriorityQueue<>(Collections.reverseOrder()).

questions.question: Why is peek() O(1) but poll() O(log n) on a PriorityQueue?
questions.answer: peek() simply reads the root, which always holds the smallest (or largest, for a max-heap) element -- no work needed. poll() removes the root and then must reorganize the heap to restore its ordering property, which takes O(log n).

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: PriorityQueue is Java's built-in heap, defaulting to a min-heap where peek() and poll() always return the smallest element -- peek() is O(1) since the smallest is always at the root, while poll() is O(log n) since removing the root requires reorganizing the heap afterward. A max-heap requires an explicit reversing Comparator, like Collections.reverseOrder(). A classic use case is finding the kth largest element by maintaining a min-heap capped at size k, discarding the smallest whenever it grows past that -- the root ends up being exactly the kth largest survivor.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: PriorityQueue = a hospital ER queue, not a checkout line -- always treats (peek/poll) whoever's most urgent (smallest, by default), no matter arrival order. Want most-severe-first instead? Flip the ordering with a Comparator.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: Does PriorityQueue default to a min-heap or a max-heap?
How do you make a PriorityQueue behave as a max-heap?
Why is peek() faster than poll()?
How does capping a min-heap at size k help find the kth largest element?

sections.title: Last Stone Weight
sections.problemStatement: You are given an array of stone weights. Repeatedly take the two heaviest stones and smash them together -- if equal, both are destroyed; otherwise the lighter is destroyed and the heavier becomes (heavier - lighter). Return the weight of the last remaining stone, or 0 if none remain.
hints: A max-heap always gives instant access to the two CURRENT heaviest stones -- exactly this lesson's max-heap pattern using Collections.reverseOrder().

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Sort the array on every iteration to find the two heaviest stones.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A max-heap gives O(log n) access to the two heaviest stones on each round, instead of resorting the whole list.

sections.title: Kth Largest Element in a Stream
sections.problemStatement: Design a class to find the kth largest element in a stream of numbers, supporting adding new elements and retrieving the kth largest at any time.

examples.input: KthLargest(3, [4,5,8,2]); add(3); add(5); add(10); add(9); add(4)
examples.explanation: Maintaining a min-heap capped at size 3, its root is always the 3rd largest overall.

constraints: At least k elements will exist before a kth largest query is called
hints: This is exactly this lesson's findKthLargest pattern, but maintained incrementally across add() calls instead of computed once.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Store all values in a list, sort on every add() call, and read off the kth largest.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A min-heap capped at size k -- exactly this lesson's findKthLargest technique, maintained incrementally.

sections.title: Kth Largest Element in an Array
sections.problemStatement: Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return the kth largest element in the array (the kth largest in sorted order, not the kth distinct value).
hints: This IS the exact findKthLargest function from this lesson's Section 2, unmodified.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Sort the array and index directly.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A min-heap capped at size k, exactly as covered in this lesson's Section 2.

sections.title: Top K Frequent Elements
sections.problemStatement: Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return the k most frequent elements, in any order.
constraints: k is in the range [1, number of distinct elements]
hints: Count frequencies with a HashMap (Hashing lesson), then use a min-heap capped at size k -- ordered by FREQUENCY instead of value -- to keep only the k most frequent.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Count frequencies with a HashMap, then sort all entries by frequency and take the top k.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Count frequencies, then use a min-heap of size k ordered by frequency -- keeping only the k most frequent, in O(n log k) instead of O(n log n).
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the frequency map, O(k) for the heap

sections.title: K Closest Points to Origin
sections.problemStatement: Given an array of points on the X-Y plane and an integer k, return the k closest points to the origin (0, 0).

examples.explanation: Distance of [1,3] from origin is sqrt(10); distance of [-2,2] is sqrt(8). [-2,2] is closer.

hints: Compare SQUARED distances (x^2 + y^2) to avoid unnecessary sqrt calls -- squared distance preserves the same relative ordering. Then it's the same max-heap-of-size-k pattern as finding the kth largest, applied to 'closest' instead.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Sort all points by squared distance, then take the first k.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A max-heap of size k, ordered by squared distance -- keeping only the k CLOSEST points seen, discarding the farthest whenever the heap grows past k.

sections.title: Find Median from Data Stream
sections.problemStatement: Design a data structure that supports adding integers from a data stream and finding the median of all elements added so far, at any point.

examples.explanation: After [1,2], median is (1+2)/2=1.5. After [1,2,3], median is 2.

constraints: At most 5*10^4 calls to addNum and findMedian
hints: Maintain TWO heaps: a max-heap for the SMALLER half of numbers, and a min-heap for the LARGER half -- keep them balanced in size (differing by at most 1).
The median is then either the max-heap's root, the min-heap's root, or their average, depending on which heap (if either) has one more element.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Keep all numbers in a sorted list, inserting each new number into its correct sorted position.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n) per addNum (insertion into a sorted list)

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Two heaps kept balanced: a max-heap for the lower half, a min-heap for the upper half -- addNum is O(log n), findMedian is O(1).
approaches.timeComplexity: O(log n) for addNum, O(1) for findMedian

sections.title: Merge k Sorted Lists
sections.problemStatement: You are given an array of k linked-lists, each sorted in ascending order. Merge all the linked-lists into one sorted linked list and return it.
hints: A min-heap holding the CURRENT front node of each list -- always pop the smallest, add it to the result, then push that list's next node (if any) back in.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Merge Two Sorted Lists (Recursion category / Linked List category) applied repeatedly, merging the result with each subsequent list one at a time.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(k * n), where n is the total number of nodes

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A min-heap holding one node per list -- always extract the smallest, append it, and push its list's next node back into the heap.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n log k), where n is the total number of nodes
